coredata.dc:title: Highly transparent, low haze and ultraviolet to blue light blocking cellulose films tailored by lutein as electronic screen protectors

coredata.dc:description: Anti-blue light films with high transparency and low haze that completely originate from renewable and biodegradable biomass materials for eye protection is a growing trend with great demand in the near future. Therefore, in this study, a natural bioactive ingredient, lutein, was incorporated into the cellulose matrix via a green and facile adsorption strategy to prepare biodegradable ultraviolet (UV) and blue light filters. The lutein/carboxymethylated cellulose films (L-CCFs) displayed high anti-UV capacity, with UV-A and UV-B blocking ratios of 52.7 %-90.9 % and 44.1–85.2 %, respectively. The composite films could block 83.1–97.8 % of blue light in the 400−500 nm region while maintaining high transparency (86.6–89.3 %) and low haze (2.1–2.6 %). By light absorption, the films can effectively shield blue light emitted by light-emitting diodes, sunshine, lighting systems, mobile phone, and computer screens. Quite encouragingly, the addition of lutein significantly increased the water contact angle from 40.1 to 70.5° and the composite films have excellent liquid barrier properties. Meanwhile, these films exhibit higher light stability than lutein powders, thus greatly extending the service life of the film as a blue light shielding material. Moreover, the films have excellent mechanical strength (>110 MPa) and biodegradability. These films prepared entirely from natural resources offer great application value in the fields of electronic screen films and spectacle lenses.

head.ce:title: Highly transparent, low haze and ultraviolet to blue light blocking cellulose films tailored by lutein as electronic screen protectors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Light Industry and Textiles, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar, Heilongjiang Province 161006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Light Industry and Textiles, Qiqihar University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Light Industry and Textiles, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar 161006, Heilongjiang Province, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Anti-blue light films with high transparency and low haze that completely originate from renewable and biodegradable biomass materials for eye protection is a growing trend with great demand in the near future. Therefore, in this study, a natural bioactive ingredient, lutein, was incorporated into the cellulose matrix via a green and facile adsorption strategy to prepare biodegradable ultraviolet (UV) and blue light filters. The lutein/carboxymethylated cellulose films (L-CCFs) displayed high anti-UV capacity, with UV-A and UV-B blocking ratios of 52.7 %-90.9 % and 44.1–85.2 %, respectively. The composite films could block 83.1–97.8 % of blue light in the 400−500 nm region while maintaining high transparency (86.6–89.3 %) and low haze (2.1–2.6 %). By light absorption, the films can effectively shield blue light emitted by light-emitting diodes, sunshine, lighting systems, mobile phone, and computer screens. Quite encouragingly, the addition of lutein significantly increased the water contact angle from 40.1 to 70.5° and the composite films have excellent liquid barrier properties. Meanwhile, these films exhibit higher light stability than lutein powders, thus greatly extending the service life of the film as a blue light shielding material. Moreover, the films have excellent mechanical strength (>110 MPa) and biodegradability. These films prepared entirely from natural resources offer great application value in the fields of electronic screen films and spectacle lenses.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: White-light-emitting diodes possessing many advantages such as small size, low heat generation, high efficiency, longer lifetime, and excellent energy conservation have been used in lighting systems, computers, cellular phones, and many other fields (Huang et al., 2015; Oh et al., 2015; Song et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2017). However, it is becoming increasingly apparent that exposure to this artificial light is harmful to the eyes. The high-energy blue light range of 400–500 nm may cause the most damage to the retinal pigment epithelium and induce the development of the disease (Jaadane et al., 2017; Lin et al., 2017; Wenzel et al., 2005). Hence, it is necessary to effectively decrease the hazard of blue light while providing a comfortable visual experience by developing materials or approaches that can block UV and blue light, and simultaneously maintain high transparency and low haze. To overcome these issues, some work has been carried out to screen the harmful UV and blue light.

It is reported that there are two effective ways to screen harmful UV and blue light. One method is to use multilayer thin film filters to reflect specific wavelengths of blue light; using two materials with different refractive indices to prepare multilayer thin films is the easiest method to design these filters (Beydaghyan et al., 2018; Li and Xu, 2020; Park et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2013). Nevertheless, these approaches for fabricating thin films are costly and require huge amounts of thin layers. Meanwhile, the accuracy of the thickness of the deposited layer is required to be very high. Another effective strategy is to use a variety of materials to absorb UV and blue light. For example, benzophenone (Corrêa et al., 2012) and organic pigment yellow (Su et al., 2021) have been used as UV and blue light absorbers. ZnO, CdS, NiOx, TiO2, and other inorganic UV or blue light absorbers were widely used in the form of nanoparticles, and satisfactory UV and blue light shielding performance was realized (Huang et al., 2019b; Kaur et al., 2021; Li and Xu, 2020; Shafei and Abou-Okeil, 2011; Subbiah et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019). Nevertheless, these absorbers have the disadvantages of heavy metal contamination and their high photocatalytic effect is harmful to the substrates (Niu et al., 2018; Sirviö et al., 2016). Most of the traditional hazardous blue light shielding materials are based on petroleum-based polymers, which are difficult to degrade, upgrade, or pollute the environment after scrapping (Devadiga and Ahipa, 2021). In recent years, hazardous light shielding films based on natural degradable polymers, such as cellulose, have received much attention (Feng et al., 2017; Parit et al., 2018; Qi et al., 2021). Given the sustainable, non-toxic, environment-friendly, and biodegradable characteristics of cellulose-based film materials, they have been considered the most promising candidate materials to replace petroleum-based productions applied in light-blocking areas. Compared with many plastic substrates, the coefficient of thermal expansion of cellulose films is much lower (Sadeghifar et al., 2017). Therefore, cellulose film can be used as a suitable carrier in combination with non-toxic, efficient UV and blue light absorbers to prepare hazardous light filters. However, it is difficult to completely avoid the dangers of UV and blue light while maintaining consistent display or lighting quality. For these reasons, there is still a need to manufacture UV and blue light-screening film materials with excellent optical properties such as high transparency and low haze to protect human health, especially to prevent damage to the eyes.

Lutein is a lipophilic substance, a carotenoid, which is an oxygen-containing derivative of isoprene polymers and is abundant in flowers, vegetables, fruits, and other plants (Ahmadzadeh and Ubeyitogullari, 2023; Jv et al., 2023). As one of the most common carotenoids, lutein is essential for human organs because of its antioxidant effects and can prevent various diseases. Lutein has a variety of functions, such as reducing the risk of age-related macular degeneration and cataracts, as well as filtering high-energy blue light (Wang et al., 2023). Moreover, lutein has the advantage of non-toxicity. However, due to its conjugated olefin structure, lutein is also sensitive to oxygen, light, and other environments such as pH and heat, thus limiting its retention (Xiao and Ahn, 2022). Due to the instability of lutein, embedding lutein into some matrices for its protection is urgently needed. As far as we know, the fabrication of cellulose films containing lutein as UV and blue light screening films to interrupt eye damage caused by electronics and artificial light sources has not been systematically studied and reported.

In this work, by integrating cellulose and lutein obtained entirely from natural resources, an efficient UV and blue light shielding film was constructed for eye protection. The hypothesis is that lutein can endow the film with the capability to shield UV and blue light, loading lutein through the cellulose matrix can improve the photostability of lutein, while the cellulose film still maintains high visible light transparency and low haze. The lutein/carboxymethylated cellulose films (L-CCFs) displayed high anti-UV capacity and irradiation stability, and UV-A and UV-B blocking ratios are 52.7–90.9 % and 44.1–85.2 %, respectively. The composite films could block 83.1–97.8 % of blue light in the 400−500 nm region while maintaining high transparency (86.6–89.3 %) and low haze (2.1–2.6 %). By light absorption, the films can effectively shield blue light emitted by light-emitting diode, sunshine, lighting system, mobile phone and computer screens. The addition of lutein significantly increased the water contact angle from 40.1 to 70.5°. Moreover, these films have good biodegradability and liquid barrier properties, and retain high tensile strength. These composite films offer advantages in the choice of raw material, the extent of UV and blue light screening, transparency, and haze.

Cotton-derived circular quantitative filter papers were purchased from Hang Zhou Special Paper Industry Co., Ltd., China. Sodium chloroacetate was acquired from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd, China. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), anhydrous ethanol, and urea were acquired from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China. 1-allyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride (AmimCl) and lutein were acquired from Aladdin Industrial Co., Ltd and were used as received.

40 g of filter paper was added in 1000 mL 1 M solution of sodium chloroacetate fabricated in 15 % (w/v) sodium hydroxide with stirring at 50ºC. After 2 h, the fibers were washed with distilled water to the neutral condition and dried at 65ºC for subsequent reactions and instrumental analysis. The methylene blue method was applied to determine the carboxyl group content of virgin and carboxymethylated cellulose fibers (Li et al., 2020). To prepare carboxymethylated cellulose hydrogel, 0.4 g fibers were added into a vessel containing 19.6 g AmimCl and then placed in a vacuum-drying oven. Subsequently, the mixture is then heated and stirred at 100ºC to obtain a transparent solution with 2.0 % cellulose concentration. The carboxymethylated cellulose solution without bubbles was cast into a glass tank and placed for a while, then immediately coagulated in distilled water. Finally, carboxymethylated cellulose hydrogel (1.2 mm of thickness) was obtained after the carboxymethylated cellulose hydrogel was repeatedly washed with distilled water to remove residual ionic liquid.

To remove water from the carboxymethylated cellulose hydrogel, it was allowed to soak in anhydrous ethanol to exchange out the water. Lutein (0.005, 0.01, and 0.02 g) was added into anhydrous ethanol to form lutein-ethanol mixture solutions. The carboxymethylated cellulose gels were immersed in the lutein-ethanol mixture solutions for 2 h at 25ºC. The surface of lutein/carboxymethylated cellulose gels was rinsed with anhydrous ethanol. Finally, the lutein/carboxymethylated cellulose gel films were fixed on the glass plates to prevent shrinkage and dried at room temperature to acquire lutein/carboxymethylated cellulose composite films with about 0.12 mm of thickness, coded as L-CCF1, L-CCF2, and L-CCF3. Carboxymethylated cellulose film (CCF, 0.12 mm) without lutein was also fabricated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV and blue light shielding performance valuation
ce:section.ce:para: UV–vis light transmittance spectra were applied to assess the UV and blue light barrier afforded by L-CCFs. UV and blue light shielding ratios of L-CCFs were assessed using the following formulas.

where T(λ) is the average spectral transmittance of samples, dλ is the band width, and λ is the wavelength.

The performance of the prepared functional films in practical applications was verified by filtering blue light from a mobile phone screen, two computer screens, lighting system, and sunlight. The measurements were recorded through CCF and L-CCFs positioned between light sources and the OHSP350 spectrometer.

The blue light-induced color change card was used to verify the blue light shielding capability of the film. A blue light LED (460 nm) is used to excite the blue light-induced color change card without and covered by films, and photographs are taken.

The degradability of CCF and L-CCFs was evaluated by enzymatic degradation (Xia et al., 2020). The test was performed in an acetate buffer (pH=5), at 37°C, by a cellulase enzyme (0.7 U/mg). 80 mg of CCF and L-CCFs were added in 20 mL of 100 ppm cellulase enzyme-acetate buffer solution. After the incubation time was reached, the specimens were washed with deionized water and dried at 35°C. The biodegradability performance was expressed as the percentage of weight loss,

where m 0 and m d are the weight of L-CCFs before and after degradation, respectively.

The water contact angle of CCF and L-CCFs was tested with a JY-82B video contact angle tester after placing approximately 5 μL of water/oil droplets on the surface of CCF or L-CCFs using a syringe. All samples were glued to a removable sample stage before measurement and the stage was placed horizontally during the test. The contact angle was measured at 34 points per sample.

The chemical composition of samples was tested using a Nicolet Nicolet 6700 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR) in the wavelength range of 4000–500 cm−1. The crystallinity phase of samples was carried out using a D/max2200VPC X-ray diffractometer (XRD) system. The XRD data were collected from 2θ=5–50° at a scan rate of 5°/min. The scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi S4800) was applied to evaluate the cross-sectional morphologies. The sample was treated with liquid nitrogen and cut off immediately with a blade, then coated with platinum, and the cross-section structure was observed at different magnifications. The thermal stability of samples was measured using a STA449 F3 thermogravimetric analyzer. The tensile testing of the films was conducted on a UTM2203 universal testing machine according to ISO527–3–1995 (E). The UV–vis spectrophotometer (TU-1950, China) was applied to test transmittance and haze. The OHSP350 spectrometer was applied to collect electronic product parameters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental scheme process with a detailed description
ce:section.ce:para: The process for fabrication of lutein/cellulose composite films (L-CCFs) with high transparency, low haze, outstanding mechanical performance, UV and blue light shielding functions through a simple and green adsorption strategy is displayed in Fig. 1a. Although some blue light shielding films have been manufactured, it is difficult to achieve both high shielding efficiency and high optical performance. It is relatively easy to achieve high transparency in cellulose films, but it is difficult to give them very low haze. In previous work, it was found that the optical properties of cellulose film prepared from cellulose fibers treated with carboxymethylation have significant changes such as an increase in transparency and a decrease in haze (Sun et al., 2021b). Therefore, in this work, carboxymethylated cellulose fibers were fabricated to enhance the optical transmittance of composite films and decrease the haze of composite films. The content of carboxyl groups in pure and modified fibers was determined according to the methylene blue method. As with the results of previous work, the carboxyl group content enhanced from 0.0053 mmol·g−1 to 0.165 mmol·g−1. The ionic liquid was used as a solvent to produce highly transparent cellulose substrate through cellulose dissolution-regeneration, which realized raw material regeneration, non-polluting production, and solvent recycling. As presented in Fig. 1b, cellulose solution has high transparency. Subsequently, cellulose solution was transformed into cellulose hydrogel after regeneration, and cellulose-based functional films were prepared by adsorption of lutein molecules with cellulose hydrogel as the substrate. As can be observed in Fig. 1c and d, the carboxymethylated cellulose hydrogel has high transparency and low haze. Although the composite film showed a yellow color after lutein was introduced, it still has high transparency and low haze.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure, thermal, and morphology analysis of L-CCFs
ce:section.ce:para: FTIR spectra of pure cellulose fibers, carboxymethylated cellulose fibers, and CCF were collected in Fig. 2a. The original cellulose fibers exhibit two major characteristic absorption peaks at 3336 and 2899 cm–1, which were ascribed to −OH and asymmetric −CH stretching, respectively. The peaks at 1644 and 1021 cm–1 were ascribed to the stretching vibration of CO and C–O, respectively. In the spectrum of carboxymethylated cellulose fibers, the absorption peak appearing at 1590 cm–1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of COO–, confirming the carboxyl groups were successfully introduced into the cellulose skeleton (Sun et al., 2021b). The CCF obtained after cellulose dissolution exhibited two major characteristic peaks at 3298 cm–1 and 2887 cm–1, and CO (COOH) was found at 1590 cm−1. The molecular interactions between cellulose matrix and lutein were also analyzed by FTIR spectra as shown in Fig. 2b. The peaks were observed at 2920 cm−1 and 2852 cm−1 assigned to the C–H stretching vibration at the saturated hydrocarbon (Dong et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2022). Two peaks at 1024 cm−1 and 963 cm−1 are assigned to the C–O stretching vibrations and C–H bending vibration from the –CH=CH– group, respectively (Dong et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2022). The broad band centered near 3321 cm−1 in all spectra of L-CCFs was assigned to the O–H stretching of cellulose. Most of the peaks in the L-CCFs were similar to those in the spectra of the CCF, although the intensities of peaks were different. A possible scheme of interactions between lutein and cellulose macromolecule is presented in Fig. 2c. The peak positions of the functional groups in the spectra of the L-CCFs did not change obviously from those of the cellulose, confirming that the introduction of lutein did not change the structure of the cellulose. The XPS spectra of CCF and L-CCF3 are shown in Fig. S1. As shown in Fig. S1a, CCF and L-CCF3 contain only carbon and oxygen elements, and the characteristic peaks of C1s and O1s are located at 284.8 eV and 532.1 eV, respectively. As shown in Fig. S1b and c, there is no significant change in the C1s and O1s high resolution spectra of L-CCF3 compared to CCF, which demonstrated that only physical interactions occurred between the cellulose matrix and the lutein molecules.

The crystalline structure of lutein, CCF, and L-CCFs was analyzed by X-ray diffraction. As displayed in Fig. 2d, the XRD pattern of CCF exhibits three typical diffraction peaks at 12.4°, 20.2°, and 22.2°, assigning to the (1−10), (110), and (020) faces of the cellulose Ⅱ crystal (Ahmed et al., 2019). It has been reported in many literature that lutein is highly crystalline (Kaya et al., 2022). As can be seen in Fig. 2d, pure lutein powder showed specific crystallographic diffraction peaks at 2θ=6.6, 8.2, 12.9, 14.0, 15.7, 17, 20.5, and 22.1°, which were consistent with the XRD spectrum of lutein in the literature (Kaya et al., 2022). However, the incorporation of lutein did not have a distinct influence on the crystalline structure of cellulose film.

To verify the thermal stability of L-CCFs, their thermal properties were evaluated by thermogravimetry analysis. The primary thermograms of lutein, CCF, and L-CCFs are presented in Fig. 2e. It was observed that all samples exhibited three stages of weight loss over a temperature range of 30–600ºC. The first stage was found at approximately 30–160ºC, a slight mass loss was observed for CCF and L-CCFs, which was ascribed to the evaporation of water from CCF and L-CCFs. The second stage (252–355ºC) is the main weight loss stage, accounting for 53.2 % of the total weight loss, which is mainly due to the degradation of the polymer chain. The third stage begins at around 355ºC and ends at 600ºC, which is attributed to the decomposition of the carbonaceous substances. The temperature at the maximum degradation rate for lutein is about 334.6ºC, which is lower than CCF (338.8ºC). All L-CCFs were thermally stable up to 355ºC, which is close to CCF. Compared with CCF, pure lutein has a lower degradation starting temperature and maximum degradation temperature. However, its addition did not have an obvious influence on the thermal stability of L-CCFs. All L-CCFs were thermally stable up to 335ºC, which is the same for regenerated CCF. These observations demonstrate that all L-CCFs in this work can also be used at elevated temperatures.

To assess the dispersion of the lutein component in L-CCFs, the cross-sectional SEM images of CCF and L-CCFs with different lutein contents were observed and the SEM images are illustrated in Fig. 2f. As displayed in Fig. 2f1, the cross-section images of pure CCF are roughly uniform, dense, and continuous. As shown in Fig. 2f2-f4, the addition of lutein did not evidently influence the cross-sectional images of the L-CCFs, demonstrating a homogeneous distribution of lutein in the cellulose films. Even with the highest lutein concentration, no visible aggregation or dispersed particles of lutein can be observed in the cross-sectional images of the L-CCFs. These results demonstrate that lutein has good interaction and compatibility with cellulose.

Fig. 3a shows photos of CCF and L-CCFs. The “Qiqihar University” on the background is visible through the CCF and L-CCFs, which indicates that CCF and L-CCFs are highly transparent. Incorporation of lutein into the cellulose films resulted in a yellow color of L-CCFs, and the content of lutein in the carboxymethylated cellulose hydrogels gradually increased with the increase in the concentration of lutein solution, which led to a gradual deepening of the color of the L-CCFs. Fig. 3b displays the UV–vis transmittance curves of CCF and L-CCFs. CCF exhibits extremely high visible light transmittance (94.1 % transmittance at 600 nm). Despite its high transparency, CCF provides little shielding effect on UV and blue light. The shielding ratios of CCF for UV-B, UV-A, and blue region are 12.5 %, 9.1 %, and 7.9 %, respectively (Fig. 3c). Fig. 3d and e show the visible light transmittance of CCF and L-CCFs in comparison with UV-A, UV-B, and blue light screening performance. With the increase of lutein content, UV-A, UV-B, and blue light transmission decreased significantly, while the visible light transmittance remained high. Specifically, L-CCF1 can shield 44.1 % of UVB, 52.7 % of UVA, and 83.1 % of blue light region. L-CCF1 has a light transmittance of up to 89.3 % at 600 nm. The UV shielding ability of L-CCF was further enhanced with increasing lutein loading. L-CCF2 can shield about 66.9 % of UVB, 70.5 % of UVA, and 82.6 % of blue region. The transmittance of L-CCF2 is still as high as 88.7 %. The UV shielding capacity of L-CCF3 increased significantly with increasing lutein loading. The blue light shielding ability of L-CCFs in the 400–500 nm range is also gradually enhanced. L-CCF3 can shield about 85.2 % of UVB, 90.9 % of UVA, and 97.8 % of blue region. Table S1 shows the visible light transmittance (600 nm) of L-CCFs in comparison with UV-A, UV-B, and blue light screening capacity. These films effectively screen most of the UV and blue light, and have sufficiently high visible light transparency. The remarkable UV-shielding capability of L-CCFs was verified by the fluorescent pattern of banknotes (Fig. 3f). When the banknote was covered with CCF without lutein, the fluorescence code can be observed, which indicates that CCF could not effectively shield UV rays. However, when the banknotes were blocked by L-CCFs, the intensity of the fluorescence code significantly weakened until disappeared, which demonstrates that L-CCFs have outstanding UV-blocking capacity. Based on the above results, it can be confirmed that the cellulose-based functional films fabricated from lutein-decorated natural cellulose have strong anti-UV performance. Table S2 displays a comparison of the transmittance and UV-shielding capability of L-CCF3 with film materials reported in other literature (Wang et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2020; X. Gu et al., 2022; Q. Gu et al., 2022; Song et al., 2021; Sun et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2021; Mehta and Kumar, 2019; Trifol and Moriana, 2022; Zheng et al., 2021). This suggests that L-CCFs have better comprehensive optical properties (UV blocking and optical transmittance) compared to other films. For practical applications, the stability of UV and blue light shielding performance of L-CCFs is crucial for long-term application. Hence, the lutein powder and L-CCF3 were placed under a UV light source (WFH-204B, 365 nm) for 20 days and measured by the UV–vis spectrophotometer and OHSP350 spectrometer after a long period of constant irradiation. The test method is shown in Fig. S2a. As displayed in Fig. S2b, after prolonged exposure to UV light, the color of the lutein powder changed significantly, which indicates the degradation of lutein, whereas the color of the film barely changed, which indicates that lutein is stable in the film. As displayed in Fig. S2c and d, L-CCF3 consistently maintained good UV and blue light blocking performance under continuous UV light irradiation. At the same time, we evaluated the stability of the composite film in high-temperature (90ºC), acidic (HCl, 0.5 mol/L), and alkaline (NaOH, 1 mol/L) liquids for 2 h (Fig. S3). After acid and alkali soak, and heat treatment, the composite film still has excellent blue light shielding performance, which shows that the composite film has excellent stability under various conditions. Table S3 shows a comparison of the blue light-shielding performance and transparency of L-CCF3 with other harmful light blocking materials (Chang et al., 2021; Han et al., 2022; Li et al., 2021; Park et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2021b; Yang et al., 2019, 2020). This indicates that L-CCF3 possesses good UV and blue light-shielding capacity compared with other thin films reported in previous works.

Photos of blue laser diode (LD, 405 nm), glass-shielded blue LD, CCF-shielded blue LD, and L-CCFs shielded blue LD were collected using the test approach displayed in Fig. S4a. Fig. S4b exhibits the photographs of the glass, CCF, and L-CCFs under a blue LD irradiation. When the blue LD is used to excite the glass and CCF, the observed light intensity is slightly weaker than that of the blue LD, which demonstrates that pure CCF and glass are not effective in shielding harmful blue light. However, since most of the blue light is efficiently absorbed, the intensity of blue light covered by L-CCF1 is significantly reduced. For L-CCF2 and L-CCF3, the intensity of blue light decreases with an increase of the lutein, which can be observed with the naked eye.

Next, anti-blue light test cards were used to measure the capability of L-CCFs to shield blue light. As is shown in Fig. 4, when blue light is used to illuminate the anti-blue light test card, the blue light test card turns from white to blue. When the L-CCFs are removed from the anti-blue light test card, it can be observed that the area covered by the L-CCF is white, which indicates that the blue light irradiated onto the anti-blue light test card is absorbed by the L-CCFs, that is, the blue light is absorbed by lutein in the film.

To verify the practicability of the L-CCFs as harmful blue light screening films, CCF and L-CCFs are applied to cover a blue LED (460 nm). Fig. 5a displays photos of CCF and L-CCFs on the blue LED. As can be seen from Fig. 5a, when CCF is covered on the blue LED, the harsh blue light can be observed. In the case of the L-CCF1, L-CCF2, and L-CCF3, the blue light intensity of the blue LED is weaker than that of the CCF. Fig. S5 displays the utilization of L-CCFs as mobile phone screen protector for eye protection. Meanwhile, the existence of L-CCF3 covers on mobile phone screen did not affect the recognition of the images.

In general, full-color displays in electronics consist of blue (B), green (G), and red (R) LEDs (Guo et al., 2022). Here, the composite films are expected to effectively block harmful UV and blue light while being able to pass through other wavelengths of visible light. Therefore, three R, G, and B LEDs were used to verify the selective blue light blocking reality effects of L-CCFs. As can be seen from Fig. 5b, the composite films have opposite filtering performance for R, G, and B LEDs. It is noteworthy that the green and red light emitted from the G and R LEDs remained almost unchanged when the G and R LEDs were shielded with L-CCF1, indicating that L-CCF1 is highly selective in cutting off the harmful blue light without affecting other light in the visible region.

Finally, L-CCFs were tested for their practicability to block harmful blue light emitted by lighting lamp ( Fig. 6), mobile phone screen ( Fig. 7), notebook computer screen (Fig. S6), desktop computer screen (Fig. S7), and sunshine (Fig. S8). The intensity spectrum of blue light blocked by CCF and L-CCFs was tested. The shielding capability of the film on the blue light emitted by the lighting lamp was first investigated, as shown in Fig. 6. It can be observed that despite the presence of CCF, the intensity of blue light is not significantly reduced, which indicates that pure CCF cannot effectively shield blue light emitted by the lighting lamp. When L-CCF1 is used to shield lighting lamp, a significant reduction in the intensity of the blue light region can be observed, which indicates that L-CCF1 has excellent blue light shielding capability. The performance of L-CCFs in shielding blue light emitted from lighting lamp gradually improved as the content of lutein in the cellulose film increased. Specifically, when L-CCF2 and L-CCF3 are used to shield the blue light emitted by the lighting lamp, it is observed that the blue light is almost completely shielded.

With the increasing level of technology, people have started using smartphones and laptops frequently. Prolonged viewing of mobile phone electronic screens exposes the eyes to blue light, which can easily cause eye damage, especially causing macular degeneration. As shown in Fig. 7, Fig. S6 and Fig. S7, CCF has little shielding effect on blue light emitted from mobile phones and computers (notebook and desktop computers), which can be demonstrated by the fact that there is no significant change in the intensity of blue light emitted by mobile phones and laptops after using CCF. When using L-CCF1 to shield mobile phone and computer screens, the intensity of blue light is significantly reduced and blue light is shielded by L-CCF1. As the concentration of lutein increases, L-CCF3 blocks almost all of the harmful blue light emitted by screens of mobile phone and notebook computer. Meanwhile, it can also be seen from Fig. S8 that L-CCFs have a good shielding effect on blue light emitted by sunlight.

Next, the haze of L-CCFs is analyzed. Generally, the lower the haze, the better, because electronic screen protectors with low haze can provide clearer visual effects and will not affect the screen display. As shown in Fig. 8a and b, with the increase of lutein content, the transmittance of the film decreased slightly, and the haze increased slightly. Buildings viewed and photographed through L-CCFs were visible, indicating that L-CCFs have high transparency and low haze that the introduction of lutein does not significantly affect the optical transmittance and haze of the films (Fig. 8c). Compared with other light barrier films that have been described, L-CCFs offer not only high transparency but also low haze, while blocking blue light more effectively. The result indicates that both CCF and L-CCF1 have lower haze than cellulose paper, anisotropic wood film, nanopaper, anisotropic BC film, isotropic BC film, CNC/CNF nanopaper, CBP, ACFs and similar to glass (Jia et al., 2017; Sun et al., 2021a; Tang et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2011; Yao et al., 2016; Ye et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2017). Compared with traditional petroleum-based materials, L-CCFs have lower haze, which indicates that L-CCFs have the potential to replace petroleum-based materials (Fig. 8e) (Xu et al., 2016). These results prove that L-CCFs with tunable blue light shielding, superior optical transmittance, and low haze can be applied as harmful blocking materials to protect our eyes. Meanwhile, L-CCFs can also be used as lenses for sunglasses to block eye damage caused by sunshine, electronic products, and lighting lamps.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water contact angle and liquid barrier performance of L-CCFs
ce:section.ce:para: It is common practice to use materials in aqueous systems. Therefore, in some cases, the materials must be liquid resistant. The high liquid barrier performance of cellulose-based paper or film materials is highly desirable in practical applications such as screen protectors for electronic products and food packaging. As displayed in Fig. 9a and b, the water contact angle (WCA) value of the CCF film is 40.1 ± 0.5° less than 90°. The L-CCF1, L-CCF2, and L-CCF3 showed WCA of 61.6 ± 0.5°, 66.2 ± 0.5°, and 70.5± 0.5°, respectively, demonstrating that the hydrophobicity of L-CCF was enhanced with the introduction of lutein. As shown in Fig. 9c-f, the L-CCFs have a strong barrier performance to water, milk, soy sauce, and plant-derived cooking oil. The hydrophilic or hydrophobic of the filler can significantly affect the wettability of the composite film (Sahraee et al., 2017). Therefore, compared with CCF, the increase in hydrophobicity of L-CCFs was mainly attributed to the hydrophobicity of lutein.

The mechanical properties are one of the important parameters of films, which are greatly affected by the internal structure of films. An important indicator that cellulose films can replace synthetic plastics is that cellulose films should have sufficient mechanical strength. Fig. S9 shows the stress-strain curves for CCF and L-CCFs. The corresponding tensile strength and Young’s modulus are presented in Fig. 10a and b. The CCF has an average maximum tensile strength and Young’s modulus of 111.1 MPa and 3.8 GPa, respectively. The average maximum tensile strength and Young’s modulus of L-CCFs are similar to those of CCF. These results demonstrated that the introduction of lutein had no significant influence on the tensile deformation behaviors. As shown in Fig. 10c, the photos show that the L-CCF3 is remarkably flexible and can be bent, twisted, folded, and knotted at will, which facilitates its use in practice. Mechanical analyses showed that the tensile strength of all films exceeded 110 MPa, which was much higher than the value of other polymeric materials (high-density polyethylene (HDPE), polystyrene (PS), acrylonitrile butadiene styrene (ABS), polycaprolactone (PCL), polyhydroxyalkanoates (PHAs), polybutylene succinate (PBS), polyvinyl chloride (PVC), polylactic acids (PLAs), and butylene adipate-co-terephthalate (PBAT)) used for producing optical materials (Fig. 10d) (Chandra et al., 2019; Cheng et al., 2018; García-Quiles et al., 2019; Kumar et al., 2010; Labet and Thielemans, 2009; Mahmoud et al., 2019; Puchalski et al., 2018; Zheng et al., 2007). Meanwhile, L-CCFs also have a higher tensile strength than some UV and blue light shielding films (CDs@cel, ANFs/NFC-9 %, HEC/ANF1.0, CNSP-X5, CA/Cu-MOF7.0, CMC/Pal-D1–9 %, C-3PMIA, CWNF-4, and NCD@cel3) (Fig. 10e) (Barman et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2021; Gong et al., 2023; Huang et al., 2019a, 2021; Ling et al., 2020; Luo et al., 2019; Sirviö et al., 2020; Xia et al., 2022). The tensile strength change of cellulose film under different humidity was tested. Fig. S10 shows the tensile strength of CCF and L-CCF3 at different relative humidity. The results showed that the tensile strength of CCF and L-CCF3 decreased continuously with increasing relative humidity. Compared with CCF, L-CCF3 has a higher tensile strength at high humidity.

As displayed in Fig. S11, the degradability of L-CCFs and CCF is expressed as a percentage of mass loss. It can be observed from Fig. S11 that the percentage of mass loss of all samples gradually increases as the cellulase treatment time increases. After the cellulase incubation time reaches 12 h, the weight losses of CCF, L-CCF1, L-CCF2, and L-CCF3 were 51.8 %, 51.2 %, 50.1 %, and 48.9 %, respectively. Moreover, after the cellulase incubation time reached 36 h, the mass loss of CCF, L-CCF1, L-CCF2, and L-CCF3 exceeded 85 %. The existence of lutein hardly affects the degradation of the L-CCFs by cellulase. The above experimental results indicate that CCF and L-CCFs have outstanding biodegradability.

Lutein, a natural molecule, is used to confer cellulose films excellent light shielding function. After the adsorption of lutein molecules, the composite films exhibit favorable features including high transparency, low haze, light-screening, and liquid barrier. This cellulose-based harmful light shielding film could be scaled-up using a simple process. A potential engineering production line of light-screening cellulose films (L-CCFs) is proposed via a continuous roll-to-roll system, as shown in Fig. 11, which comprises the fabrication of carboxymethylated cellulose fibers, cellulose solution, cellulose gel film, adsorption of lutein via simple soaking, and finally, washing and drying. The potential use of L-CCFs as light shielding materials is displayed in the form of protective films for electronic products (Fig. S5). In summary, this highly scalable, low-cost, and green preparation process confers the functional cellulose film with great potential for practical use.

In this work, the lutein-containing cellulose films with high mechanical strength, biodegradability, liquid barrier, and tunable UV and blue light-blocking properties were prepared by a sustainable, facile, and scalable method. The incorporation of lutein enabled the cellulose films to block UV-A and UV-B as well as blue light meanwhile retaining high transmittance (86.6–89.3 %) and low haze (2.1–2.6 %). By light absorption, the films can effectively screen blue light emitted by sunshine, lighting systems, computer, and mobile phone screens. The results show that these films can effectively reduce UV and blue light damage while having a good visual experience. Meanwhile, these films exhibit high light stability, thus greatly extending the service life of the film as a blue light shielding material. Quite encouragingly, the addition of lutein significantly increased the water contact angle from 40.1 to 70.5°. Moreover, the films have excellent good mechanical strength (>110 MPa), liquid-barrier, and biodegradability. Above all, a simple, green, and scalable technique has been developed to prepare lutein-tailored UV and blue light screening cellulose-based film materials, which have high potential to be used in electronic screen protectors.
